coredata.dc:title: GQDs@SiO3Pr Schiff-base complexes Co (II); As an eco-friendly environmental nanocatalyst for the synthesis of the amino carbonitrile chromenes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Behrouzi, Marziyeh
Rabiei, Khadijeh
Ghasemzadeh, Soheil
coredata.dc:description: An effective nanocomposite comprising functionalized graphene quantum dots has been developed for the rapid, one-step production of amino carbonitrile chromenes. To this end, the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was functionalized with a novel synthetic Schiff-base complex of cobalt (II) prepared by post-synthetic surface modification of the graphene quantum dot surface with a silane coupling agent (3-chloropropyl trimethoxysilane, SiO3PrCl) in order to synthesize GQDs@SiO3PrCl. Then, the Schiff base ligand synthesized from the condensation reaction of 4-aminoantipyrine with ortho-phenylenediamine was immobilized on the surface of the silane-linker grafted GQDs to synthesize the GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base. Finally, the cobalt particles were stabilized on the surface of the graphene quanta functionalized with Schiff bases (GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff Base/Co (II)). The GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff Base/Co (II) nanocatalyst demonstrated exceptional performance in the synthesis of amino carbonitrile chromenes derivatives, facilitating high product yields using various aromatic aldehydes, 4‑hydroxy coumarin and malononitrile under mild conditions. Additionally, the catalyst exhibited reusability up to seven times without significant loss of productivity, showcasing its environmental friendliness and sustainability in organic synthesis. This versatile nanocatalyst holds promising for efficient and economically viable catalysis in diverse chemical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff-base/Co (II)
Nanocatalyst, Amino carbonitrile chromenes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff base complexes co(ii)(8)
Preparation of GQDs (2) using citric acid (CA)
Synthesis of functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs@SiO3PrCl) (3)
Preparation of Schiff base functionalized GQDs (7)
Synthesis of a cobalt (II)-Schiff base complex functionalized quantum dots (GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/co(ii))(8)
Typical procedure for the synthesis of 2-amino-4-aryl-5-oxo-4,5-dihydropyrano[3,2-c]chromene-3-carbonitrile compounds in the presence of GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff-base/Co (II)
The purpose mechanism of amino carbonitriles preparation in the presence of GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/Co(II) nanocatalyst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff base complex Co (II)(8).
The color of GQDs under UV light irradiation.
FT-IR spectroscopy for GQDs (A), GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff base (B) and GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff base Co (II) complex (C).
X-ray diffraction (XRD) for GQDs (A) and GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff base/Co (II) nanocatalyst(B).
TGA analyses for GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/Co (II) complex.
FESEM images of GQDs (A, B) and GQDs@SiO3Pr Schiff-base Co (II) complex (C, D), and TEM image of GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff base Co (II) complex (E, F).
Distribution diagram for GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff-base Co(II)complex nanocatalyst particles.
The EDX analysis of GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/Co (II) complex.
EDS-mapping analysis of GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/Co (II) nanocatalyst.

Substrate scope Reaction conditions: 1 mmol 1, 1 mmol 2 and 1 mmol 3.
Recycling of GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff base Co(II) complex nanocatalyst in the model reaction.
XRD (A) and FT-IR (B) of GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff base Co(II) complex nanocatalyst.
Chemicals and equipment used in this study. Scanning Electron Microscopy.

row.entry: Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Energy-dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis
Bruker DRX-400 spectrometer in DMSO solvent and TMS as the internal reference

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Reaction conditions: 1 mmol 3, 1 mmol 4, and 1 mmol 5a, 6 mL solvent, 30 min.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of some reported catalysts for the synthesis of dihydropyrano[3,2-c]chromenes.

head.ce:title: GQDs@SiO3Pr Schiff-base complexes Co (II); As an eco-friendly environmental nanocatalyst for the synthesis of the amino carbonitrile chromenes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Qom University of Technology, Qom, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Qom University of Technology, Qom, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An effective nanocomposite comprising functionalized graphene quantum dots has been developed for the rapid, one-step production of amino carbonitrile chromenes. To this end, the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was functionalized with a novel synthetic Schiff-base complex of cobalt (II) prepared by post-synthetic surface modification of the graphene quantum dot surface with a silane coupling agent (3-chloropropyl trimethoxysilane, SiO3PrCl) in order to synthesize GQDs@SiO3PrCl. Then, the Schiff base ligand synthesized from the condensation reaction of 4-aminoantipyrine with ortho-phenylenediamine was immobilized on the surface of the silane-linker grafted GQDs to synthesize the GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base. Finally, the cobalt particles were stabilized on the surface of the graphene quanta functionalized with Schiff bases (GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff Base/Co (II)). The GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff Base/Co (II) nanocatalyst demonstrated exceptional performance in the synthesis of amino carbonitrile chromenes derivatives, facilitating high product yields using various aromatic aldehydes, 4‑hydroxy coumarin and malononitrile under mild conditions. Additionally, the catalyst exhibited reusability up to seven times without significant loss of productivity, showcasing its environmental friendliness and sustainability in organic synthesis. This versatile nanocatalyst holds promising for efficient and economically viable catalysis in diverse chemical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, there has been a significant focus on developing organic synthesis methods that are safer and more sustainable by utilizing the principles of green chemistry [1-4]. This approach entails the use of ecologically friendly chemicals, solvents, durable catalysts, and efficient methods. Heterogeneous catalytic processes have gained increasing interest in recent decades [5-9]. Creating heterogeneous catalysts using carbon-based material, particularly renewable biopolymers, is a feasible method for producing biodegradable solid catalysts for heterogeneous catalysis [10].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are an important kind of carbon-based nanomaterials with exceptional properties, including size-dependent optoelectronic characteristics, high surface area, and environmental friendliness [11,12] Recently, citric acid [13-15], glucose, l-glutamic acid [16], Focus racemose leaves [17], nutmeg seeds [18], corn powder, neem leaves, rice husks, rice grains, coffee grounds, and honey [19] have all been utilized as starting materials. These materials are versatile materials with unique optical, electrical, and chemical properties that make them suitable for a wide range of applications, especially in catalysis. Their synthesis can be achieved through various top-down and bottom-up methods, and their properties can be tailored through surface functionalization and doping to enhance performance in specific applications [20].

Chemical functionalization, polymer grafting, doping, and hybridization are some methods for the functionalization of GQDs [21,22]. Functionalized GQDs can serve as efficient catalysts in photocatalytic water splitting, CO2 reduction, and H2 production, in coupling reactions between CO2 and propylene oxide which have been well-summarized in several reviews [23-26], and can be used in bio sensing, fuel cells, and environmental applications due to the high surface area and conductive properties.These findings highlight the versatility and potential of GQDs in various applications [27-29]. GQD-based catalysts enable environmentally sustainable and cost-effective reaction processes by optimizing their reusability and preventing catalyst depletion.

Multicomponent reactions are synthetic methods that consist of a one-pot reaction in which three or more substrates are combined, resulting in the formation of organic molecules that are structurally diverse and complex [29-32]. Attention has been drawn to these reactions on account of their substantial applications, as they are especially advantageous in the synthesis of natural products and molecules with biological significance [5,33-35]

Cheromen derivatives have gained the attention of scientists due to their different applications in medicine. They have different biological activities such as anticonvulsant, antimicrobial, anticancqer, anti-tuberculosis, anti-cholinesterase, anti-diabetic, and inhibitor of mono amine oxidase activities [36-39]. In addition, they exhibited anti-inflammatory, antiviral, anti-hypertensive, anti-tuberculous, anti-adipogenic, anti-Hyperglycemia, pharmacological activities, antioxidant, neuroprotective actions and muscle relaxants [40-41].

These significant therapeutic properties have motivated chemists to create ecologically friendly, cost-efficient, and effective catalytic methods for producing chromene compounds. This study developed a cost-effective and eco-friendly catalyst for the synthesis of chromenes using GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff Base/Co (II) nanocomposite (Scheme 1 ). In this regard, a novel functionalized GQDs with Schiff base Co (II) complexes was synthesized. This innovative method synthesizes these compounds with over 95% efficiency and is ecologically friendly. The catalyst's ability to be reused and recycled benefits both the environment and economy. A variety of approaches were used to examine the nanocomposite composition. The reaction employed in this study is eco-friendly and non-polluting. This nanocatalyst effectively merges the positive properties of acid-based catalysts, easy separation and a large surface area.
All the chemicals, reagents, and equipment used in this study are listed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff base complexes co(ii)(8)
Preparation of GQDs (2) using citric acid (CA)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) involved using high-purity citric acid monohydrate (98%) and sodium hydroxide (97%) obtained from Merck Germany. To synthesize GQDs, citric acid (8 g, 41.6 mmol) was placed in a flask and heated on a hot plate at 190 ℃ for 25 min until it turned a dark orange color. Subsequently, the solution was allowed to cool, and a sodium hydroxide solution with a concentration of 0.5 M was gradually added until the pH reached 8. The resulting solution was then dried at 70 ℃, and the resulting product was used in subsequent stages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs@SiO3PrCl) (3)
ce:section.ce:para: This process involves the functionalization of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with chlorine, which can serve as a valuable means for introducing new functional groups to facilitate further chemical reactions. Initially, synthetic GQDs (4 g) were transferred into a 25-mL round-bottom flask. Subsequently, 8 mL of dichloromethane was added to the flask. The resulting mixture was stirred using a magnetic stirrer for 10 min at room temperature. In the subsequent step, 16 mL (0.08 mmol) of 3-chloropropyl trimethoxysilane was added to the flask. The reaction mixture was then heated to 60 ℃ and stirred magnetically for 20 h. After completion of the reaction, the contents of the flask were filtered and subsequently washed with 5 mL of chloroform. Finally, the product was dried in an oven at 90 °C.

This process involves the chemical reaction between 4-aminoantipyrine and ortho-phenylenediamine. First, 4-aminoantipyrine (16.7 mmol, 3.4 g) and 10 mL of DMF were added to a flask and stirred. Then, ortho-phenylenediamine (8.36 mmol, 0.9 g), dissolved in DMF, was added to the contents of the flask. The mixture in the flask was heated to 90 ℃ and stirred magnetically for 24 h. The progress of the reaction was monitored using TLC. After the reaction was complete, the contents were extracted and recrystallized using EtOH (6 mL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Schiff base functionalized GQDs (7)
ce:section.ce:para: For this experiment, synthetic GQDs@SiO3PrCl (3 g) were dispersed in EtOH (6 mL) and stirred for 5 min. The synthesized Schiff base 6 (3.3 g) was dissolved in 6 mL of EtOH and added to the reaction flask. The mixture was then stirred at reflux for 24 h. After that, the reaction mixture was filtered, washed with 5 mL of CHCl3, and dried in an oven at 90 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of a cobalt (II)-Schiff base complex functionalized quantum dots (GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/co(ii))(8)
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs functionalized (2 g) with a Schiff base (7) was transferred to a flask and dispersed in to ethanol (10 mL) for 15 min. Next, cobalt nitrate (2 g, mmol) dissolved in ethanol was added to the flask. The reaction mixture was then refluxed at 80 ℃ and stirred magnetically for 24 h. After completion of the reaction, desired products GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/Co (II) were washed exactly with EtOH (8 mL) and dried at 90 °C in an Oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Typical procedure for the synthesis of 2-amino-4-aryl-5-oxo-4,5-dihydropyrano[3,2-c]chromene-3-carbonitrile compounds in the presence of GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff-base/Co (II)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic nanocatalyst GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/Co (II) (5 mg), 4-Clbenzaldehyde (1 mmol), 4‑hydroxy coumarin (1 mmol), and malonitrile (1 mmol) were added to a flask equipped with a magnetic stirrer. The mixture was heated at 80 °C in a solvent mixture of EtOH:H2O (1:1). To monitor the progress of the reaction, TLC (ethyl acetate/petroleum: 2/3) was used. The reaction mixture was then washed with DCM (3 mL x 3) to separate the product from the nanocatalyst. Finally, the desired product (4b) was purified using hot EtOH (5 mL).
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were successfully synthesized. The synthetic protocol of the nanocatalyst GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/Co (II) is shown in Scheme 1.
The structure was confirmed qualitatively by the solution color at the end of the reaction. The GQDs solution appeared green under Ultra Violate (UV) light (Fig. 1 ).

The GQDs solution was identified using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Fig. 2 shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum, which indicates peaks at 320 nm. According to Karimi and Ramezanzadeh [42]. GQDs have maximum wavelengths of 227 nm and 360 nm. This suggests electronic transitions of π–π* and n–π* vibrations, respectively.

The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy of GQDs, GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base, and GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/Co (II) are represented in Fig. 3 (A, B, C). The absorption band observed at the wavenumber of 3348 cm-1 is caused by the stretching vibration of the OH group in the structure (Fig. 3-A). Other significant peaks typical for GQDs are also observed, including vibration peaks at wavenumbers 1565 and 1414 cm−1, which are assigned to the aromatic C = C and COH at 1284 cm−1 [13,42].. This observation suggests that citric acid (CA) pyrolysis and partial carbonization were performed to obtain GQDs. By comparing spectrum, A, B, and C, it can be confirmed that the functionalization of graphene quantum dots has occurred. The absorption band appearing at wavenumber 1650 cm-1 is related to the asymmetric stretching vibration of C = N, which proves the functionalization of GQDs using Schiff base [43]. The presence of peaks at wavenumbers 1264 cm-1 and 1024 cm-1 is related to the stretching vibration of CN and Si-O bonds (Fig. 3-B) [44]. Furthermore, the absorption band appearing at wavenumber 540 cm-1 confirms the formation of the Schiff base complex, specifically the stretching vibration of Co-N [45]. Fig. 3-C clearly shows the synthesis of modified GQDs with the Schiff base complex. All of these peaks exhibit blue shifts as Co comes in the complex in GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff base Co (II) complex catalyst, indicating the successful formation of the Co complex (Fig. 3C).

The XRD patterns of GQDs and GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff base Co (II) complex are shown in Fig. 4 (A, B). As shown in Fig. (4-A) spectrum a relatively wide characteristic peak at 2Ɵ=32.04°, which assigned to the plane with the Miller index (002) and corresponds to card number (JCPD 1487–41) in carbon materials [46]. The (002) peak confirmed the carbonization of citric acid and the interlayer spacing of the GQDs structure [47]. Also, no other specific peaks are seen in this spectrum, and this indicates that the synthetic GQDs is pure. The average particle size of the sample was estimated according to Scherer's formula was 0.154 nm. The obtained value was in agreement with the SEM results of synthetic GQDs. The XRD pattern of the prepared GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff base Co (II) complex catalyst shows characteristic peaks of GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff base Co (II) complex center at 2θ = 31.86°, and Cobalt characteristic peaks at 45.62°, 56.60°and 66° with miller indexes (400), (422) and (533) (In accordance to PDF 43–1004). Hence, these observations prove the successful conjugation of Schiff base ligand and complexation of Cobalt species on the functionalized GQDs surface (Fig. 4-B).

Fig. 5 indicates that GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff base/Co (II) exhibited a three-step decomposition, resulting in a weight loss of 45% at temperatures of 64.15, 185.27, and 377.27 °C, respectively. The removal of water within the structure contributed to an 8% weight loss between temperatures of 80 °C and 100 °C. Furthermore, a notable weight loss of 37% occurred between 100 and 440 °C, indicating the decomposition of the organic framework and confirming the functionalization of GQDs (Fig. 5).

The surface parameters of GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/Co (II) nanocatalyst were determined using the N2 adsorption/desorption technique. The results of the N2 adsorption/desorption are depicted in Fig. 10 . The adsorption isotherm demonstrated mesoporous structure and a type III behavior [50]. The area and total pore volume were calculated as 4.9937 m2 g-1 and 0.021723 cm3 g-1, respectively. Additionally, the average pore diameter was measured as 17.4 nm.

Different parameters were examined to identify the most suitable conditions for the model reaction (Table 2 ). The model reaction for chromene synthesis was studied with 4-hydroxycumarin (1 mmol), 4-chlorobenzaldehyde (1 mmol), and malonitrile (1 mmol) as substrates in the presence of GQDs@SiO3PrSchiff base/Co (II) as nanocatalyst, As can be seen in Table 2, with the use of 0.005 g of functionalized GQDs nanocatalyst, the yield of the desired product has increased to 80% (entry 6), and the increase of more than this amount of nanocatalyst has not affected the product yield. In the absence of a catalyst, only a 5% yield was obtained even though the reaction continued for 8 h, justifying the need to use the catalyst mentioned in this reaction study (entry 1).

The role of solvents in the reaction was also screened, and it was found that a mixture of H2O/EtOH in a ratio of 1:1 is the best solvent to produce the target product 4a (entry 12). The effect of temperature on the desired product yield was investigated. At 80 °C, the chosen product 4a yield increased to 97%. In addition, further increasing the reaction temperature slightly decreased the product formation time (entry 17).

Due to confirmation of the generality, various benzaldehyde derivatives bearing electron-donating and withdrawing groups 3a-l were selected and reacted with 4‑hydroxy coumarin 1 and malonitril 2 using GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base /Co(II) complex under optimized reaction conditions (Scheme 2 ). Using GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/Co(II) complex, the desired products 4a-l were obtained with excellent yields of up to 98% and quickly. In addition, the yield of the products (4e and 4i) increased to the highest value in the presence of benzaldehyde derivatives with electron-withdrawing groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The purpose mechanism of amino carbonitriles preparation in the presence of GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/Co(II) nanocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 3 , the carbonyl and nitrile groups in the aldehyde and malononiitrile are activated through interaction with the nanocatalyst. This accelerate the condensation reaction of aldehyde with malononitrile leading to the quick formation of the desired alpha-beta unsaturated intermediate (I). Additionally, the use of this nanocatalyst accelerate Michael addition reaction of 4‑hydroxy coumarine with alpha-beta unsaturated intermediate (I) and intra-molecular cyclization to synthesize intermediate (II). As a result, the desired product, amino carbonitrile chromene, was obtained [51].

The catalytic performance of the GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/Co(II) complex has been compared to that of other catalysts in similar research papers. The results of these surveys are presented in Table 3 . Comparing the GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/Co(II) complex nano catalyst with other catalysts, it becomes evident that the reaction exhibits greater efficiency and a faster kinetic rate. Moreover, this innovative nano catalyst is characterized by its stability, non-toxicity, economic feasibility, ease of synthesis, and ease of separation from the reaction. The notable advantages of this approach include operational simplicity, environmentally friendly reaction conditions, a straightforward and cost-effective procedure, high efficiency, short reaction times, and the ability to be reused for multiple cycles. This method also demonstrates tolerance towards substrates containing both electron-donating and electron-withdrawing functionalities.

The reusability of heterogeneous catalysts is a crucial advantage, especially in industrial applications. In this study, we focused on exploring the recovery and reuse potential of the synthesized functionalized GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base-Co(II) nanocatalyst. The findings of this investigation are illustrated in Fig. 11 . In order to accomplish our goal, we performed the synthesis of 4a using the GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/Co(II) nanocatalyst. After the completion of the reaction, the nanocatalyst 4 was separated through centrifugation and washed with dichloromethane (10 mL). Following drying, the nanocatalyst was reused for subsequent reactions. This process was repeated seven times, and the yield of the desired product 4a was determined for each iteration. The results indicate that the reused GQDs@SiO3Pr-Schiff base/Co(II) nanocatalyst exhibited satisfactory reaction efficiency (Fig. 11).

To further validate the structural integrity of the catalyst after reuse, FT-IR and XRD analysis was utilized (Fig. 12 ). The FT-IR and XRD analysis of the catalyst after seven cycles, revealed the stable structure that were consistent with those of the fresh catalyst, thereby confirming the catalyst's reusability.

In summary, a newly developed green nanocomposite was synthesized by functionalization of GQDs with a novel synthetic Schiff-base complex of cobalt(II). In this regard, a novel Schiff base ligand synthesized from the condensation reaction of aminoantipyrine with ortho-phenylenediamine. This robust and heterogeneous nanocatalyst demonstrated exceptional efficacy in the synthesis of amino carbonitrile chromene derivatives. The nanocatalyst exhibited notable efficiency in activation of both electron-donating and electron-withdrawing aryl benzaldehydes, yielding the respective products with excellent yields. Importantly, the study of the reusability of this synthetic nanocatalyst revealed that up to 7 times reuse of the catalyst formed the desired product in excellent yield without a decrease in its effectiveness.

Marziyeh Behrouzi: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Khadijeh Rabiei: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Soheil Ghasemzadeh: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express appreciation to the Qom University of Technology for supporting this investigation.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jorganchem.2024.123413.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Catalytic application of a novel melamine–naphthalene-1,3-disulfonic acid metal–organic framework in the synthesis of β-acetamido ketones

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Click synthesis of 1,2,3-triazoles using copper iodide nanoparticles anchored poly(sulfonamide-thiazole) modified layered double hydroxides/chitosan nanocomposite

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of benzothiazoles using an iron-anchored polysulfonamide modified layered double oxide/sodium alginate nanocomposite

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CuI nanoparticle-immobilized on a hybrid material composed of IRMOF-3 and a sulfonamide-based porous organic polymer as an efficient nanocatalyst for one-pot synthesis of 2,4-diaryl-quinolines

Ghorbani-Vaghei, CuI nanoparticle-immobilized on a hybrid material composed of IRMOF-3 and a sulfonamide-based porous organic polymer as an efficient nanocatalyst for one-pot synthesis of 2,4-diaryl-quinolines, RSC. Adv.., 13 (2023) 11480–11494.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of copper iodide nanoparticles immobolized porous polysulfonamide gel as an effective nanocatalyst for synthesis of aminoindolizines
